content.assessment: No international comparator for a passive demographic-contraction baseline is supplied; this is a counterfactual/no-policy scenario rather than a policy mechanism, so the international-comparison lens has limited direct applicability. Indirectly, the Polish case is relevant as a cautionary analogy: uncoordinated capacity shifts (there, from a reversal rather than demographic decline) produced severe unplanned placement shortfalls (F-live-international_comparison-07), suggesting that even demographically-driven, non-policy contraction could generate similar transition frictions if left uncoordinated, a risk the proposal itself flags.
opportunities: Uncoordinated demographic contraction could be compared post hoc to the Polish transition-shortfall pattern (F-live-international_comparison-07) to extract early-warning indicators for capacity mismatches
strengths: Passive contraction, being agnostic to admission criteria, avoids directly triggering the coaching/instrument-design distortions relevant to T1, and is not a comparative-policy mechanism subject to the transferability critique applied to active reforms
threats: The Polish case demonstrates that even reasonably foreseeable capacity transitions, if unmanaged, can produce large-scale unplaced-student shortfalls (F-live-international_comparison-07); passive, unmanaged demographic contraction carries an analogous risk profile absent active coordination
weaknesses: No comparable international demographic-contraction case (with or without policy coordination) is present in the evidence base to validate outcome relevance

Passive contraction does nothing to address the internationally-flagged structural drivers of Hungary's exceptional inequality—early tracking's between-school concentration (F-live-international_comparison-02) and its ethnic-segregation entanglement (F-live-international_comparison-06)—so even if it shrinks track size, the comparative evidence suggests the inequality-generating mechanism itself would persist unchanged
